stem: Let \( p(x) = x^{3} + ([[a]])x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). By the Remainder Theorem, what is the remainder when \( p(x) \) is divided by \( x - ([[r]]) \)?
solution: By the Remainder Theorem, the remainder on division by \( x - ([[r]]) \) is \( p([[r]]) = ([[r]])^{3} + ([[a]])([[r]])^{2} + ([[b]])([[r]]) + ([[c]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( p(-[[r]]) \), as if dividing by \( x + ([[r]]) \) instead of \( x - ([[r]]) \).
This drops the constant term \( ([[c]]) \) when evaluating \( p([[r]]) \).
This reports the constant term of \( p \) rather than \( p([[r]]) \).
This is off by one from the correct remainder.
This is off by one from the correct remainder.
This is off by two from the correct remainder.

stem: The polynomial \( p(x) = x^{3} + ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])x + c \) has \( x - ([[r]]) \) as a factor, for some constant \( c \). What is \( c \)?
distractor_rationales: This forgets to negate the sum, leaving \( p([[r]]) \) itself set equal to \( c \) rather than solving \( p([[r]])=0 \) for \( c \).
This drops the linear term \( ([[B]])([[r]]) \) before negating.
This computes the correct expression but adds an extra 1 before negating.
This drops the cubic term entirely, using only the linear contribution.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: The cubic \( p(x) = x^{3} + ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \) has \( x = [[r]] \) as one of its roots. What is the sum of its other two roots?
solution: By Vieta's formulas, the sum of ALL three roots is \( -([[A]]) \). Subtracting off the known root \( [[r]] \) leaves the sum of the other two: \( -([[A]]) - ([[r]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports the sum of all three roots, forgetting to subtract off the known root \( [[r]] \).
This forgets to negate the coefficient \( [[A]] \) before subtracting the known root.
This forgets to negate the coefficient \( [[A]] \) and also adds \( [[r]] \) instead of subtracting it.
This swaps the order and sign of the two quantities being combined.
This is off by one from the correct sum.
This is off by one from the correct sum.

stem: According to the Rational Root Theorem, how many candidate rational roots does \( p(x) = x^{3} + ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \) admit?
solution: Since \( p \) is monic, every candidate rational root is \( \pm d \) for a positive divisor \( d \) of the constant term \( [[C]] \). The number of positive divisors of \( |[[C]]| \) is \( \text{divisor\_count}(|[[C]]|) \), and doubling for the \( \pm \) sign gives \( [[answer]] \) candidates.
distractor_rationales: This forgets to double the divisor count to account for both positive and negative candidates.
This adds one to the (undoubled) divisor count instead of doubling it.
This doubles correctly but then subtracts one for no valid reason.
This doubles correctly but then adds two extra spurious candidates.
This overcounts by two.
This undercounts by two.

stem: Let \( p(x) = x^{3} + ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])x + ([[C]]) \). What is the remainder when \( p(x) \) is divided by \( [[m]]x - ([[n]]) \)?
solution: The linear factor \( [[m]]x - ([[n]]) \) vanishes at \( x = \dfrac{[[n]]}{[[m]]} \), so by the Remainder Theorem the remainder is \( p\!\left(\dfrac{[[n]]}{[[m]]}\right) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( p \) at the coefficient \( [[m]] \) itself instead of at the actual root \( [[n]]/[[m]] \).
This evaluates \( p \) at the coefficient \( [[n]] \) itself, forgetting to divide by \( [[m]] \).
This inverts the fraction, using \( [[m]]/[[n]] \) instead of \( [[n]]/[[m]] \) as the root.
This reports the constant term of \( p \) rather than \( p([[n]]/[[m]]) \).
This is off by one from the correct remainder.
This is off by one from the correct remainder.

stem: The polynomial \( p(x) = x^{3} + ([[A]])x^{2} + ([[B]])x + k \) leaves a remainder of \( [[R]] \) when divided by \( x - ([[r]]) \), for some constant \( k \). What is \( k \)?
distractor_rationales: This adds the remainder instead of subtracting the other terms from it.
This ignores the given remainder \( [[R]] \) entirely, solving as if the remainder were 0.
This negates the correct value (a sign slip when isolating \( k \)).
This reports the given remainder itself instead of solving for \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
